question: Tell me about yourself.

stories.title: Builder With Range
stories.situation: I am Emmanuel Acheampong, a Computer Science student at Grambling State University graduating in May 2027.
stories.task: I have used internships, projects, and leadership work to grow beyond classroom knowledge.
stories.action: I built backend systems at Geaux Network and Hubtel, shipped projects in machine learning and visualization, and stayed active in NSBE and ColorStack.
stories.result: That mix has made me a fast learner who likes building software with clear user impact.

stories.title: Impact Focus
stories.situation: Most of my work has centered on solving practical problems for users or teams.
stories.task: I look for opportunities where technical depth leads to measurable outcomes.
stories.action: I worked on a hospital pilot that cut time to treatment, a data pipeline that improved downtime detection, and a learning tool used by more than 1000 students.
stories.result: The common thread is that I like software that makes a real difference.

stories.title: Student And Community Builder
stories.situation: Alongside engineering work, I care about growth and community.
stories.task: I wanted to pair technical progress with leadership and service.
stories.action: I helped grow NSBE at Grambling, stayed active in ColorStack, and kept strong academics while building projects outside class.
stories.result: That has shaped me into someone who enjoys learning fast and helping teams move forward.

question: Tell me about a time you had a disagreement with your manager.

stories.title: Geaux Scope Alignment
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, engineering and content teams wanted different things from the Change History View.
stories.task: I needed to land a version that solved the traceability problem without letting the feature sprawl.
stories.action: I brought both groups into one discussion, mapped the highest value event sources, and proposed a phased release.
stories.result: We shipped a useful first version and kept both teams aligned.

stories.title: Hubtel Test Debate
stories.situation: At Hubtel, I felt our initial test coverage plan was too light for transaction related workflows.
stories.task: I needed to disagree without slowing the team down through friction.
stories.action: I showed concrete failure cases, added more backend tests, and explained the reliability tradeoff clearly.
stories.result: The team expanded coverage and the service was much more dependable.

stories.title: NSBE Planning Tension
stories.situation: In NSBE, officers sometimes disagreed on whether to prioritize events, outreach, or sponsor work first.
stories.task: My role was to reduce tension and keep momentum.
stories.action: I summarized what each person wanted, tied the discussion back to member value, and built a more balanced plan.
stories.result: That kept collaboration healthy and helped the chapter keep growing.

question: Tell me about a situation when you had a conflict with a teammate.

stories.title: Geaux Scope Alignment
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, engineering and content teams wanted different things from the Change History View.
stories.task: I needed to land a version that solved the traceability problem without letting the feature sprawl.
stories.action: I brought both groups into one discussion, mapped the highest value event sources, and proposed a phased release.
stories.result: We shipped a useful first version and kept both teams aligned.

stories.title: Hubtel Test Debate
stories.situation: At Hubtel, I felt our initial test coverage plan was too light for transaction related workflows.
stories.task: I needed to disagree without slowing the team down through friction.
stories.action: I showed concrete failure cases, added more backend tests, and explained the reliability tradeoff clearly.
stories.result: The team expanded coverage and the service was much more dependable.

stories.title: NSBE Planning Tension
stories.situation: In NSBE, officers sometimes disagreed on whether to prioritize events, outreach, or sponsor work first.
stories.task: My role was to reduce tension and keep momentum.
stories.action: I summarized what each person wanted, tied the discussion back to member value, and built a more balanced plan.
stories.result: That kept collaboration healthy and helped the chapter keep growing.

question: Tell me about a time you failed. How did you deal with the situation?

stories.title: MedDiagnose Accuracy Miss
stories.situation: On MedDiagnose AI, the stroke risk model came in at 45 percent accuracy, below what I initially hoped for.
stories.task: I had to be honest about the gap and still help the team move forward responsibly.
stories.action: I shared the results early, revisited the data and framing, and positioned the tool as decision support rather than overclaiming.
stories.result: The pilot still reduced time to treatment by 35 percent and taught me a lot about responsible product framing.

stories.title: Compiler Rework
stories.situation: While building my mini compiler, I moved quickly and discovered stability issues after adding more features.
stories.task: I needed to recover without defending a weak implementation.
stories.action: I tightened the parser and optimization flow and added better tests before moving forward.
stories.result: The compiler became more reliable and I learned not to let speed outrun correctness.

stories.title: Underestimating Integration
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, I initially underestimated how much integration work the service layer would require.
stories.task: I had to recover without creating confusion for the team.
stories.action: I broke the work into smaller milestones, checked assumptions earlier, and used more end to end tests.
stories.result: We still delivered successfully and I became better at estimating hidden complexity.

question: Describe a time when you led a team. What was the outcome?

stories.title: Growing NSBE
stories.situation: When I took on leadership work in NSBE at Grambling, there was a chance to make the chapter more useful for students.
stories.task: I wanted to build a stronger technical community and create more opportunity.
stories.action: I organized around student needs and supported outreach that led to company partnerships.
stories.result: The chapter grew to more than 300 members and secured about 3000 dollars in support.

stories.title: Leading MedDiagnose
stories.situation: MedDiagnose had product, backend, frontend, and ML pieces moving at once.
stories.task: I needed to keep the team aligned while also contributing technically.
stories.action: I drove progress on the higher risk parts, clarified ownership, and kept the pilot goals central in our decisions.
stories.result: We launched the system and completed a real pilot in Louisiana.

stories.title: Driving VisualizeMyAlgorithm
stories.situation: VisualizeMyAlgorithm started as a project, but I treated it like a real product for learners.
stories.task: That meant leading through product and engineering decisions rather than just coding features.
stories.action: I prioritized the most educational visualizations, built reusable components, and kept the experience coherent as scope expanded.
stories.result: The platform reached more than 1000 learners and became a strong example of leadership through execution.

question: Tell me about a time you worked well under pressure.

stories.title: Tight Geaux Release
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, some releases combined frontend work, service integration, access control, and testing in a short window.
stories.task: I had to keep quality high while moving quickly.
stories.action: I focused on blockers first, worked the critical path one piece at a time, and kept risks visible.
stories.result: We shipped successfully and I reinforced a calm, structured approach under pressure.

stories.title: Hubtel Reliability Pressure
stories.situation: At Hubtel, the systems I worked on supported transaction heavy services, so backend reliability mattered immediately.
stories.task: I needed to move fast without guessing.
stories.action: I relied on logs, targeted validation, and careful testing around the event flow.
stories.result: That work contributed to a 40 percent improvement in downtime detection and stronger reliability overall.

stories.title: Balancing Multiple Commitments
stories.situation: There were periods when I was balancing school, leadership, and engineering work all at once.
stories.task: The challenge was staying consistent without letting one area collapse quietly.
stories.action: I time blocked heavily, ranked priorities weekly, and communicated early when tradeoffs were needed.
stories.result: That helped me stay strong academically while still delivering in projects and organizations.

question: Provide an example of a time when you had to make a difficult decision.

stories.title: Phased Rollout Choice
stories.situation: While building the Change History View, there were more possible sources and UI details than we could safely ship at once.
stories.task: I had to choose between chasing completeness and shipping something useful.
stories.action: I chose a phased rollout focused on the highest value traceability paths first.
stories.result: That kept the release realistic and still delivered clear value.

stories.title: Responsible Product Framing
stories.situation: When MedDiagnose did not hit the accuracy level I initially wanted, we had to decide how to position the system.
stories.task: The key decision was whether to delay everything or frame it more responsibly.
stories.action: I pushed for using it as decision support with clear human oversight.
stories.result: That preserved trust and still allowed a valuable hospital pilot.

stories.title: Architecture At Hubtel
stories.situation: At Hubtel, we needed an architecture that could handle high volume events without slowing critical workflows.
stories.task: I had to support a choice that balanced present delivery with long term scale.
stories.action: I helped build around Kafka Connect, MQTT, and PostgreSQL because that combination matched the throughput and reliability needs.
stories.result: The result was a scalable pipeline with strong operational impact.

question: Describe a time when you went above and beyond the requirements for a project.

stories.title: Beyond Scope At Geaux
stories.situation: My main work at Geaux centered on product and service delivery, but I also saw gaps in logging, accessibility, and access control.
stories.task: No one had explicitly asked me to own all of that.
stories.action: I added interaction logging, supported accessibility testing, and implemented RBAC protections for key endpoints.
stories.result: Those additions improved the product and showed that I like to solve the full problem, not just the ticket.

stories.title: Preventive Mindset At Hubtel
stories.situation: While working on the Hubtel event pipeline, I noticed we could ship functionality without being fully confident in its resilience.
stories.task: I wanted to strengthen the system before that turned into avoidable incidents.
stories.action: I pushed for better tests and treated reliability as part of delivery instead of post delivery cleanup.
stories.result: That mindset contributed to a major improvement in backend reliability.

stories.title: Self Directed Product Build
stories.situation: VisualizeMyAlgorithm started because I saw how much easier concepts became once students could watch them unfold.
stories.task: Nobody assigned me that problem.
stories.action: I designed and built the platform from scratch, focusing on clarity, modularity, and performance.
stories.result: It became a useful product for more than 1000 learners.

question: How do you handle a situation where you don't know the answer to a question?

stories.title: Learning gRPC Fast
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, I had to contribute in Java, gRPC, and Protocol Buffers in a production setting.
stories.task: I needed to ramp quickly enough that my learning curve did not slow the team.
stories.action: I studied existing patterns, asked focused questions, and reinforced my understanding through end to end testing.
stories.result: I became productive quickly and shipped meaningful backend work.

stories.title: Adapting To Event Systems
stories.situation: At Hubtel, I was working with Kafka Connect, MQTT, and PostgreSQL in a real time pipeline.
stories.task: That required a stronger systems mindset than typical classroom work.
stories.action: I learned by tracing the full event flow, validating assumptions, and treating debugging as part of the learning process.
stories.result: That helped me deliver the pipeline and made me much more comfortable with event driven systems.

stories.title: Learning Through Building
stories.situation: I often learn best by building projects that force me to understand a concept end to end.
stories.task: I wanted to turn that habit into a real advantage.
stories.action: That is why I built projects in machine learning, visualization, and compiler design instead of staying with only familiar tools.
stories.result: Those experiences made me much more adaptable when new technologies appear.

question: Describe a time you received tough or critical feedback.

stories.title: Maintainability Feedback
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, I got feedback that an early version of my service layer work was technically correct but harder to maintain than it needed to be.
stories.task: I needed to take that seriously without becoming defensive.
stories.action: I asked follow ups, simplified the design, and improved the tests so the intent was easier to trust.
stories.result: The result was cleaner code and better judgment around maintainability.

stories.title: Model Reality Check
stories.situation: In MedDiagnose, I had to hear that the model performance did not support the confidence I initially wanted to have in it.
stories.task: That was hard because I had invested a lot in that part of the system.
stories.action: I treated the feedback as a signal to revisit the data, the framing, and the product expectations.
stories.result: It made me more evidence driven and more honest about technical limits.

stories.title: Leadership Feedback
stories.situation: In NSBE, I got feedback that I was trying to carry too much of the work myself.
stories.task: I needed to hear that because good intent still does not scale.
stories.action: I responded by delegating more clearly and making ownership more visible across the team.
stories.result: That improved execution and made me a better collaborator.

stories.title: Maintainability Feedback

stories.result: It made me more evidence driven and more honest about technical limits.

stories.action: I responded by delegating more clearly and making ownership more visible across the team.
stories.result: That improved execution and made me a better collaborator.

question: Tell me about a time when you had to prioritize your tasks quickly.

stories.title: Geaux Task Ranking
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, I often had frontend tasks, backend work, and testing needs competing for attention.
stories.task: I had to decide what actually moved the project forward.
stories.action: I prioritized work that unblocked others, handled risky integration points early, and left lower risk cleanup for later.
stories.result: That helped me stay effective across multiple responsibilities.

stories.title: Academics And Leadership
stories.situation: Between school, NSBE, and personal projects, I regularly have more opportunities than time.
stories.task: The challenge is making tradeoffs intentionally rather than overcommitting.
stories.action: I rank work by impact, deadlines, and long term payoff, then time box the rest.
stories.result: That has helped me maintain strong academics while still producing meaningful engineering and leadership work.

stories.title: Urgent Versus Important
stories.situation: At Hubtel, urgent reliability issues could appear while longer term work was still in flight.
stories.task: I had to respond to the immediate need without losing the strategic thread.
stories.action: I treated operational issues as top priority when core workflows were affected, then returned to planned work with clear notes and next steps.
stories.result: That balance helped me contribute to both stability and long term improvements.

question: Describe a time when you anticipated potential problems and developed preventive measures.

stories.title: Consolidating Event Sources
stories.situation: The Change History View at Geaux required combining multiple backend event sources into one coherent story for users.
stories.task: The real problem was not just data display but understandable traceability.
stories.action: I shaped the events into more consistent forms and built the UI around clarity instead of raw volume.
stories.result: The feature became far more useful for tracking edits and metadata changes.

stories.title: Tracing The Full Event Path
stories.situation: In the Hubtel pipeline, small issues upstream could create much larger operational symptoms downstream.
stories.task: I needed to solve the whole system problem, not just the first visible symptom.
stories.action: I traced the path from ingestion to storage to notification and validated assumptions through tests and logs.
stories.result: That systems view helped improve downtime detection and overall reliability.

stories.title: Solving The Learning Problem
stories.situation: With VisualizeMyAlgorithm, many learners struggled because they could not see state changes over time.
stories.task: I wanted to solve the root learning problem, not just present animated output.
stories.action: I designed the experience around step by step state visibility for each algorithm.
stories.result: That helped users understand concepts faster and made the product more effective.

question: Describe a situation where you had to deal with a difficult customer or stakeholder.

stories.title: Content And Engineering Balance
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, the Change History feature sat between backend concerns and the practical needs of content users.
stories.task: I had to gather what each group needed without letting the feature grow out of control.
stories.action: I listened for the workflows each team cared about most and kept tradeoffs visible.
stories.result: That made the final feature more credible because it solved a real operational problem.

stories.title: Hospital Pilot Trust
stories.situation: MedDiagnose involved stakeholders with very different priorities, especially clinicians.
stories.task: I needed to communicate the system honestly so expectations stayed realistic.
stories.action: I focused on what the product could already do well, acknowledged limitations, and framed it as support for faster decisions.
stories.result: That built trust and helped us complete a valuable pilot.

stories.title: Sponsor Outreach
stories.situation: In NSBE, external sponsors cared about different outcomes than student members did.
stories.task: I had to represent student needs while speaking in a way companies would respond to.
stories.action: I tied our outreach to both community impact and talent development.
stories.result: That helped us secure partnerships while keeping the mission centered on students.

stories.task: I had to be honest about the gap and still help the team move forward responsibly.
stories.action: I shared the results early, revisited the data and framing, and positioned the tool as decision support rather than overclaiming.

question: How would you design or test a product to make sure it's diverse and inclusive to all users?

stories.title: Inclusive Product Thinking
stories.situation: When I think about product design, I start by asking who might be excluded by the default version of the feature.
stories.task: I want the design to work for different users and roles, not just the ideal case.
stories.action: I validate the workflow with stakeholders early, include accessibility checks, and keep permissions and clarity in mind from the start.
stories.result: That usually leads to a product that is easier to trust and easier to use.

stories.title: Healthcare Context
stories.situation: In healthcare software, inclusion is especially important because people rely on the system under different constraints.
stories.task: For MedDiagnose, the product needed to be practical for clinicians, support staff, and time sensitive workflows.
stories.action: I think about readability, role based access, alert clarity, and how the product behaves when data is incomplete.
stories.result: That creates a safer and more usable product.

stories.title: Learning Product Design
stories.situation: For an educational product like VisualizeMyAlgorithm, inclusive design means accounting for different levels of prior knowledge.
stories.task: I needed the platform to work for both beginners and more advanced learners.
stories.action: I focused on visual clarity, pacing, and step by step explanations so users did not need to infer too much from code alone.
stories.result: That made the platform more accessible to a wider range of learners.

question: Describe a time you had to explain a complex technical concept to someone non-technical.

stories.title: Explaining ML Simply
stories.situation: In MedDiagnose, I had to explain the machine learning component to people who cared more about clinical usefulness than technical detail.
stories.task: My task was to make it understandable without overselling it.
stories.action: I described it in terms of patterns in patient data, what the model could support, and where human judgment still mattered.
stories.result: That made the discussion clearer and built trust.

stories.title: Translating Event Systems
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, some of the people affected by backend events were not deeply technical.
stories.task: I needed to explain why multiple event sources mattered and how the feature would help them.
stories.action: I translated the system into simple workflow language like who changed what, when, and where they could trace it.
stories.result: That made alignment much easier.

stories.title: Teaching Through Visuals
stories.situation: VisualizeMyAlgorithm pushed me to explain technical ideas to learners.
stories.task: The challenge was making complex concepts feel intuitive rather than abstract.
stories.action: I used visual steps, comparisons, and plain language to show what each algorithm was doing.
stories.result: That helped users learn faster and reminded me that communication is core to engineering.

question: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a colleague. How did you handle the situation?

stories.title: Geaux Scope Alignment
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, engineering and content teams wanted different things from the Change History View.
stories.task: I needed to land a version that solved the traceability problem without letting the feature sprawl.
stories.action: I brought both groups into one discussion, mapped the highest value event sources, and proposed a phased release.
stories.result: We shipped a useful first version and kept both teams aligned.

stories.title: Hubtel Test Debate
stories.situation: At Hubtel, I felt our initial test coverage plan was too light for transaction related workflows.
stories.task: I needed to disagree without slowing the team down through friction.
stories.action: I showed concrete failure cases, added more backend tests, and explained the reliability tradeoff clearly.
stories.result: The team expanded coverage and the service was much more dependable.

stories.title: NSBE Planning Tension
stories.situation: In NSBE, officers sometimes disagreed on whether to prioritize events, outreach, or sponsor work first.
stories.task: My role was to reduce tension and keep momentum.
stories.action: I summarized what each person wanted, tied the discussion back to member value, and built a more balanced plan.
stories.result: That kept collaboration healthy and helped the chapter keep growing.

question: Give an example of a time you had to collaborate effectively with a team from a different department.

stories.title: Cross Functional Geaux Work
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, I worked with engineering and content teams on a feature that touched both backend events and frontend visibility.
stories.task: I needed to contribute technically while keeping everyone aligned on the problem.
stories.action: I clarified requirements early, reflected them back simply, and used testing to keep behavior aligned.
stories.result: That helped us ship a feature that felt coherent across teams.

stories.title: Multi Disciplinary MedDiagnose
stories.situation: MedDiagnose required coordination across ML, frontend, backend, and product choices.
stories.task: The team had to move together despite different strengths and viewpoints.
stories.action: I helped bridge gaps by translating constraints across disciplines and contributing where the risk was highest.
stories.result: That collaboration helped us deliver a real pilot ready system.

stories.title: Building Community Through NSBE
stories.situation: Growing NSBE meant collaborating with students from different majors, backgrounds, and goals.
stories.task: I wanted the organization to feel useful to all of them.
stories.action: I supported planning and communication around events that balanced technical growth with community building.
stories.result: That collaborative approach helped the chapter scale strongly.

question: Tell me about a complex technical project you've worked on.

stories.title: Geaux Service Layer
stories.situation: One of the most technical projects I worked on was building an API service layer at Geaux using Java, gRPC, and Protocol Buffers.
stories.task: The challenge was making backend communication reliable while keeping the contracts testable and maintainable.
stories.action: I implemented the service layer with end to end coverage and aligned it with internal podcast workflows.
stories.result: The result was stronger data accuracy for a platform serving more than 10000 monthly listeners.

stories.title: Hubtel Event Pipeline
stories.situation: At Hubtel, I worked on a real time data pipeline built around Kafka Connect, MQTT, and PostgreSQL.
stories.task: The challenge was handling high volume system events in a way that improved observability and response time.
stories.action: I built and validated the flow, added tests, and supported low latency notifications.
stories.result: That work reduced downtime detection by 40 percent and improved reliability overall.

stories.title: Mini Compiler
stories.situation: My mini compiler project was technically deep because it combined lexical analysis, parsing, optimization, and code generation.
stories.task: I wanted to understand how language tooling works beneath the surface.
stories.action: I implemented error detection, constant folding, and dead code elimination, then improved the design through repeated debugging.
stories.result: It helped more than 50 developers and sharpened my systems thinking.

question: Why are you interested in working at [company name]?

stories.title: Impact Matters Most
stories.situation: I am most motivated by teams where engineering work has visible impact on users or operations.
stories.task: That matters to me because my best experiences have combined technical depth with measurable outcomes.
stories.action: I have gravitated toward work in healthcare, creator tools, and transaction systems for that reason.
stories.result: It keeps me energized when I can see why the engineering effort matters.

stories.title: Growth Through Hard Problems
stories.situation: I am motivated by environments that force me to keep growing.
stories.task: I do my best work when the problem is real and the bar is high.
stories.action: That is why I have chosen internships and projects that stretched me into event systems, machine learning, and deeper systems work.
stories.result: I want my next opportunity to continue that pattern.

stories.title: Product And People
stories.situation: I like roles where strong engineering connects to product thinking and collaboration.
stories.task: I do not want to work in a silo.
stories.action: Experiences like Geaux Network, MedDiagnose, and NSBE showed me that I enjoy building alongside other disciplines and helping users directly.
stories.result: That combination is usually what makes a role most exciting to me.

question: Assume you are given a task to design a system. How would you do it? How would you resolve ambiguity?

stories.title: Inclusive Product Thinking
stories.situation: When I think about product design, I start by asking who might be excluded by the default version of the feature.
stories.task: I want the design to work for different users and roles, not just the ideal case.
stories.action: I validate the workflow with stakeholders early, include accessibility checks, and keep permissions and clarity in mind from the start.
stories.result: That usually leads to a product that is easier to trust and easier to use.

stories.title: Healthcare Context
stories.situation: In healthcare software, inclusion is especially important because people rely on the system under different constraints.
stories.task: For MedDiagnose, the product needed to be practical for clinicians, support staff, and time sensitive workflows.
stories.action: I think about readability, role based access, alert clarity, and how the product behaves when data is incomplete.
stories.result: That creates a safer and more usable product.

stories.title: Learning Product Design
stories.situation: For an educational product like VisualizeMyAlgorithm, inclusive design means accounting for different levels of prior knowledge.
stories.task: I needed the platform to work for both beginners and more advanced learners.
stories.action: I focused on visual clarity, pacing, and step by step explanations so users did not need to infer too much from code alone.
stories.result: That made the platform more accessible to a wider range of learners.

question: Have you ever been in a situation where another team and yours were creating a similar product? What happened?

stories.action: Experiences like Geaux Network, MedDiagnose, and NSBE showed me that I enjoy building alongside other disciplines and helping users directly.
stories.result: That combination is usually what makes a role most exciting to me.

question: Tell me a time when you had a different opinion than the rest of the team. How did you handle it?

stories.title: Phased Rollout Choice
stories.situation: While building the Change History View, there were more possible sources and UI details than we could safely ship at once.
stories.task: I had to choose between chasing completeness and shipping something useful.
stories.action: I chose a phased rollout focused on the highest value traceability paths first.
stories.result: That kept the release realistic and still delivered clear value.

stories.title: Responsible Product Framing
stories.situation: When MedDiagnose did not hit the accuracy level I initially wanted, we had to decide how to position the system.
stories.task: The key decision was whether to delay everything or frame it more responsibly.
stories.action: I pushed for using it as decision support with clear human oversight.
stories.result: That preserved trust and still allowed a valuable hospital pilot.

stories.title: Architecture At Hubtel
stories.situation: At Hubtel, we needed an architecture that could handle high volume events without slowing critical workflows.
stories.task: I had to support a choice that balanced present delivery with long term scale.
stories.action: I helped build around Kafka Connect, MQTT, and PostgreSQL because that combination matched the throughput and reliability needs.
stories.result: The result was a scalable pipeline with strong operational impact.

question: Tell me about a time when you were faced with a problem that had a number of possible solutions. What was the problem and how did you determine the course of action?

stories.title: Consolidating Event Sources
stories.situation: The Change History View at Geaux required combining multiple backend event sources into one coherent story for users.
stories.task: The real problem was not just data display but understandable traceability.
stories.action: I shaped the events into more consistent forms and built the UI around clarity instead of raw volume.
stories.result: The feature became far more useful for tracking edits and metadata changes.

stories.title: Tracing The Full Event Path
stories.situation: In the Hubtel pipeline, small issues upstream could create much larger operational symptoms downstream.
stories.task: I needed to solve the whole system problem, not just the first visible symptom.
stories.action: I traced the path from ingestion to storage to notification and validated assumptions through tests and logs.
stories.result: That systems view helped improve downtime detection and overall reliability.

stories.title: Solving The Learning Problem
stories.situation: With VisualizeMyAlgorithm, many learners struggled because they could not see state changes over time.
stories.task: I wanted to solve the root learning problem, not just present animated output.
stories.action: I designed the experience around step by step state visibility for each algorithm.
stories.result: That helped users understand concepts faster and made the product more effective.

question: Describe a time when you needed to motivate a group of individuals or encourage collaboration during a particular project.

stories.title: Growing NSBE
stories.situation: When I took on leadership work in NSBE at Grambling, there was a chance to make the chapter more useful for students.
stories.task: I wanted to build a stronger technical community and create more opportunity.
stories.action: I organized around student needs and supported outreach that led to company partnerships.
stories.result: The chapter grew to more than 300 members and secured about 3000 dollars in support.

stories.title: Leading MedDiagnose
stories.situation: MedDiagnose had product, backend, frontend, and ML pieces moving at once.
stories.task: I needed to keep the team aligned while also contributing technically.
stories.action: I drove progress on the higher risk parts, clarified ownership, and kept the pilot goals central in our decisions.
stories.result: We launched the system and completed a real pilot in Louisiana.

stories.title: Driving VisualizeMyAlgorithm
stories.situation: VisualizeMyAlgorithm started as a project, but I treated it like a real product for learners.
stories.task: That meant leading through product and engineering decisions rather than just coding features.
stories.action: I prioritized the most educational visualizations, built reusable components, and kept the experience coherent as scope expanded.
stories.result: The platform reached more than 1000 learners and became a strong example of leadership through execution.

question: What do you do to enhance your technical knowledge apart from your project work?

stories.title: Learning gRPC Fast
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, I had to contribute in Java, gRPC, and Protocol Buffers in a production setting.
stories.task: I needed to ramp quickly enough that my learning curve did not slow the team.
stories.action: I studied existing patterns, asked focused questions, and reinforced my understanding through end to end testing.
stories.result: I became productive quickly and shipped meaningful backend work.

stories.title: Adapting To Event Systems
stories.situation: At Hubtel, I was working with Kafka Connect, MQTT, and PostgreSQL in a real time pipeline.
stories.task: That required a stronger systems mindset than typical classroom work.
stories.action: I learned by tracing the full event flow, validating assumptions, and treating debugging as part of the learning process.
stories.result: That helped me deliver the pipeline and made me much more comfortable with event driven systems.

stories.title: Learning Through Building
stories.situation: I often learn best by building projects that force me to understand a concept end to end.
stories.task: I wanted to turn that habit into a real advantage.
stories.action: That is why I built projects in machine learning, visualization, and compiler design instead of staying with only familiar tools.
stories.result: Those experiences made me much more adaptable when new technologies appear.

question: How do you prioritize your workload? What do you do when your work feels like it's just too much to get done?

stories.title: Geaux Task Ranking
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, I often had frontend tasks, backend work, and testing needs competing for attention.
stories.task: I had to decide what actually moved the project forward.
stories.action: I prioritized work that unblocked others, handled risky integration points early, and left lower risk cleanup for later.
stories.result: That helped me stay effective across multiple responsibilities.

stories.title: Academics And Leadership
stories.situation: Between school, NSBE, and personal projects, I regularly have more opportunities than time.
stories.task: The challenge is making tradeoffs intentionally rather than overcommitting.
stories.action: I rank work by impact, deadlines, and long term payoff, then time box the rest.
stories.result: That has helped me maintain strong academics while still producing meaningful engineering and leadership work.

stories.title: Urgent Versus Important
stories.situation: At Hubtel, urgent reliability issues could appear while longer term work was still in flight.
stories.task: I had to respond to the immediate need without losing the strategic thread.
stories.action: I treated operational issues as top priority when core workflows were affected, then returned to planned work with clear notes and next steps.
stories.result: That balance helped me contribute to both stability and long term improvements.

question: What's the number one accomplishment you're most proud of?

stories.title: MedDiagnose Pilot
stories.situation: The accomplishment I am most proud of is helping build MedDiagnose into something that could be piloted in a real healthcare setting.
stories.task: I wanted the project to create value beyond just being a good demo.
stories.action: I contributed across the stack and helped shape a responsible pilot ready product.
stories.result: The pilot reduced time to treatment by 35 percent, which made the work especially meaningful to me.

stories.title: Growing NSBE
stories.situation: I am also proud of the impact of my work in NSBE at Grambling.
stories.task: My goal was to help students feel that engineering opportunities were real and accessible.
stories.action: Through steady organizing and outreach, I helped support a stronger community and meaningful partnerships.
stories.result: Seeing the chapter grow past 300 members felt tangible and lasting.

stories.title: Useful Tools
stories.situation: I am proud of projects like VisualizeMyAlgorithm and my mini compiler because they helped real users.
stories.task: I wanted them to be more than portfolio pieces.
stories.action: I focused on clarity, usability, and technical depth so the tools would genuinely help people learn or build.
stories.result: Reaching more than 1000 learners on one project and more than 50 developers on another showed that the work was useful.

question: Tell me about a time when you had an excessive amount of work and you knew you could not meet the deadline. How did you manage?

stories.title: Tight Geaux Release
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, some releases combined frontend work, service integration, access control, and testing in a short window.
stories.task: I had to keep quality high while moving quickly.
stories.action: I focused on blockers first, worked the critical path one piece at a time, and kept risks visible.
stories.result: We shipped successfully and I reinforced a calm, structured approach under pressure.

stories.title: Hubtel Reliability Pressure
stories.situation: At Hubtel, the systems I worked on supported transaction heavy services, so backend reliability mattered immediately.
stories.task: I needed to move fast without guessing.
stories.action: I relied on logs, targeted validation, and careful testing around the event flow.
stories.result: That work contributed to a 40 percent improvement in downtime detection and stronger reliability overall.

stories.title: Balancing Multiple Commitments
stories.situation: There were periods when I was balancing school, leadership, and engineering work all at once.
stories.task: The challenge was staying consistent without letting one area collapse quietly.
stories.action: I time blocked heavily, ranked priorities weekly, and communicated early when tradeoffs were needed.
stories.result: That helped me stay strong academically while still delivering in projects and organizations.

question: What will be your course of action if you are assigned a task you don't know at all?

stories.title: Learning gRPC Fast
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, I had to contribute in Java, gRPC, and Protocol Buffers in a production setting.
stories.task: I needed to ramp quickly enough that my learning curve did not slow the team.
stories.action: I studied existing patterns, asked focused questions, and reinforced my understanding through end to end testing.
stories.result: I became productive quickly and shipped meaningful backend work.

stories.result: That helped me deliver the pipeline and made me much more comfortable with event driven systems.

stories.situation: I often learn best by building projects that force me to understand a concept end to end.
stories.task: I wanted to turn that habit into a real advantage.
stories.action: That is why I built projects in machine learning, visualization, and compiler design instead of staying with only familiar tools.
stories.result: Those experiences made me much more adaptable when new technologies appear.

question: Give an example of when you took a huge risk and failed.

stories.title: MedDiagnose Accuracy Miss
stories.situation: On MedDiagnose AI, the stroke risk model came in at 45 percent accuracy, below what I initially hoped for.
stories.task: I had to be honest about the gap and still help the team move forward responsibly.
stories.action: I shared the results early, revisited the data and framing, and positioned the tool as decision support rather than overclaiming.
stories.result: The pilot still reduced time to treatment by 35 percent and taught me a lot about responsible product framing.

stories.title: Compiler Rework
stories.situation: While building my mini compiler, I moved quickly and discovered stability issues after adding more features.
stories.task: I needed to recover without defending a weak implementation.
stories.action: I tightened the parser and optimization flow and added better tests before moving forward.
stories.result: The compiler became more reliable and I learned not to let speed outrun correctness.

stories.title: Underestimating Integration
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, I initially underestimated how much integration work the service layer would require.
stories.task: I had to recover without creating confusion for the team.
stories.action: I broke the work into smaller milestones, checked assumptions earlier, and used more end to end tests.
stories.result: We still delivered successfully and I became better at estimating hidden complexity.

stories.situation: I often learn best by building projects that force me to understand a concept end to end.
stories.task: I wanted to turn that habit into a real advantage.
stories.action: That is why I built projects in machine learning, visualization, and compiler design instead of staying with only familiar tools.
stories.result: Those experiences made me much more adaptable when new technologies appear.

question: Tell me about a time when you had to make a decision without all the information you needed.

stories.title: Phased Rollout Choice
stories.situation: While building the Change History View, there were more possible sources and UI details than we could safely ship at once.
stories.task: I had to choose between chasing completeness and shipping something useful.
stories.action: I chose a phased rollout focused on the highest value traceability paths first.
stories.result: That kept the release realistic and still delivered clear value.

stories.title: Responsible Product Framing
stories.situation: When MedDiagnose did not hit the accuracy level I initially wanted, we had to decide how to position the system.
stories.task: The key decision was whether to delay everything or frame it more responsibly.
stories.action: I pushed for using it as decision support with clear human oversight.
stories.result: That preserved trust and still allowed a valuable hospital pilot.

stories.title: Architecture At Hubtel
stories.situation: At Hubtel, we needed an architecture that could handle high volume events without slowing critical workflows.
stories.task: I had to support a choice that balanced present delivery with long term scale.
stories.action: I helped build around Kafka Connect, MQTT, and PostgreSQL because that combination matched the throughput and reliability needs.
stories.result: The result was a scalable pipeline with strong operational impact.

question: Tell me a time when you linked two or more problems together and identified an underlying issue.

stories.title: Consolidating Event Sources
stories.situation: The Change History View at Geaux required combining multiple backend event sources into one coherent story for users.
stories.task: The real problem was not just data display but understandable traceability.
stories.action: I shaped the events into more consistent forms and built the UI around clarity instead of raw volume.
stories.result: The feature became far more useful for tracking edits and metadata changes.

stories.title: Tracing The Full Event Path
stories.situation: In the Hubtel pipeline, small issues upstream could create much larger operational symptoms downstream.
stories.task: I needed to solve the whole system problem, not just the first visible symptom.
stories.action: I traced the path from ingestion to storage to notification and validated assumptions through tests and logs.
stories.result: That systems view helped improve downtime detection and overall reliability.

stories.title: Solving The Learning Problem
stories.situation: With VisualizeMyAlgorithm, many learners struggled because they could not see state changes over time.
stories.task: I wanted to solve the root learning problem, not just present animated output.
stories.action: I designed the experience around step by step state visibility for each algorithm.
stories.result: That helped users understand concepts faster and made the product more effective.

question: Tell me about a time you made a decision to sacrifice short-term gain for a longer-term goal.

stories.title: Phased Rollout Choice
stories.situation: While building the Change History View, there were more possible sources and UI details than we could safely ship at once.
stories.task: I had to choose between chasing completeness and shipping something useful.
stories.action: I chose a phased rollout focused on the highest value traceability paths first.
stories.result: That kept the release realistic and still delivered clear value.

stories.title: Responsible Product Framing
stories.situation: When MedDiagnose did not hit the accuracy level I initially wanted, we had to decide how to position the system.
stories.task: The key decision was whether to delay everything or frame it more responsibly.
stories.action: I pushed for using it as decision support with clear human oversight.
stories.result: That preserved trust and still allowed a valuable hospital pilot.

stories.title: Architecture At Hubtel
stories.situation: At Hubtel, we needed an architecture that could handle high volume events without slowing critical workflows.
stories.task: I had to support a choice that balanced present delivery with long term scale.
stories.action: I helped build around Kafka Connect, MQTT, and PostgreSQL because that combination matched the throughput and reliability needs.
stories.result: The result was a scalable pipeline with strong operational impact.

question: How would you respond if you were the last member of the team in the office on a Friday afternoon and the product owner asks you to develop and deploy a change to production?

stories.title: Stay Calm First
stories.situation: In ambiguous situations, my first instinct is to separate real risk from emotional urgency.
stories.task: I want to avoid both overreacting and freezing.
stories.action: I gather the minimum facts, check what is reversible, and use process before improvisation.
stories.result: That usually leads to better judgment under pressure.

stories.title: Protect Trust
stories.situation: I think good judgment often means choosing the honest path even when a more optimistic story would be easier in the moment.
stories.task: I learned that clearly while working on MedDiagnose.
stories.action: I would rather frame a system responsibly and preserve trust than chase short term excitement.
stories.result: That mindset helps me make steadier decisions.

stories.title: Clarity Over Drama
stories.situation: I am usually skeptical of urgency that comes without evidence.
stories.task: Not every loud request is actually the highest priority problem.
stories.action: I look for impact, reversibility, and documented paths before making a big move.
stories.result: That helps me stay practical when situations are noisy.

question: Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

stories.title: Impact Matters Most
stories.situation: I am most motivated by teams where engineering work has visible impact on users or operations.
stories.task: That matters to me because my best experiences have combined technical depth with measurable outcomes.
stories.action: I have gravitated toward work in healthcare, creator tools, and transaction systems for that reason.
stories.result: It keeps me energized when I can see why the engineering effort matters.

stories.title: Growth Through Hard Problems
stories.situation: I am motivated by environments that force me to keep growing.
stories.task: I do my best work when the problem is real and the bar is high.
stories.action: That is why I have chosen internships and projects that stretched me into event systems, machine learning, and deeper systems work.
stories.result: I want my next opportunity to continue that pattern.

stories.title: Product And People
stories.situation: I like roles where strong engineering connects to product thinking and collaboration.
stories.task: I do not want to work in a silo.
stories.action: Experiences like Geaux Network, MedDiagnose, and NSBE showed me that I enjoy building alongside other disciplines and helping users directly.
stories.result: That combination is usually what makes a role most exciting to me.

question: What does your best day of work look like?

stories.action: I have gravitated toward work in healthcare, creator tools, and transaction systems for that reason.
stories.result: It keeps me energized when I can see why the engineering effort matters.

stories.situation: I am motivated by environments that force me to keep growing.

stories.result: I want my next opportunity to continue that pattern.

stories.title: Product And People
stories.situation: I like roles where strong engineering connects to product thinking and collaboration.
stories.task: I do not want to work in a silo.
stories.action: Experiences like Geaux Network, MedDiagnose, and NSBE showed me that I enjoy building alongside other disciplines and helping users directly.
stories.result: That combination is usually what makes a role most exciting to me.

question: What words would your colleagues use to describe you?

stories.title: Explaining ML Simply
stories.situation: In MedDiagnose, I had to explain the machine learning component to people who cared more about clinical usefulness than technical detail.
stories.task: My task was to make it understandable without overselling it.
stories.action: I described it in terms of patterns in patient data, what the model could support, and where human judgment still mattered.
stories.result: That made the discussion clearer and built trust.

stories.title: Translating Event Systems
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, some of the people affected by backend events were not deeply technical.
stories.task: I needed to explain why multiple event sources mattered and how the feature would help them.
stories.action: I translated the system into simple workflow language like who changed what, when, and where they could trace it.
stories.result: That made alignment much easier.

stories.title: Teaching Through Visuals
stories.situation: VisualizeMyAlgorithm pushed me to explain technical ideas to learners.
stories.task: The challenge was making complex concepts feel intuitive rather than abstract.
stories.action: I used visual steps, comparisons, and plain language to show what each algorithm was doing.
stories.result: That helped users learn faster and reminded me that communication is core to engineering.

question: What is something that you had to push for in your previous projects?

stories.title: Beyond Scope At Geaux
stories.situation: My main work at Geaux centered on product and service delivery, but I also saw gaps in logging, accessibility, and access control.
stories.task: No one had explicitly asked me to own all of that.
stories.action: I added interaction logging, supported accessibility testing, and implemented RBAC protections for key endpoints.
stories.result: Those additions improved the product and showed that I like to solve the full problem, not just the ticket.

stories.title: Preventive Mindset At Hubtel
stories.situation: While working on the Hubtel event pipeline, I noticed we could ship functionality without being fully confident in its resilience.
stories.task: I wanted to strengthen the system before that turned into avoidable incidents.
stories.action: I pushed for better tests and treated reliability as part of delivery instead of post delivery cleanup.
stories.result: That mindset contributed to a major improvement in backend reliability.

stories.title: Self Directed Product Build
stories.situation: VisualizeMyAlgorithm started because I saw how much easier concepts became once students could watch them unfold.
stories.task: Nobody assigned me that problem.
stories.action: I designed and built the platform from scratch, focusing on clarity, modularity, and performance.
stories.result: It became a useful product for more than 1000 learners.

question: If this were your first annual review, what would I be telling you right now?

stories.title: MedDiagnose Pilot
stories.situation: The accomplishment I am most proud of is helping build MedDiagnose into something that could be piloted in a real healthcare setting.
stories.task: I wanted the project to create value beyond just being a good demo.
stories.action: I contributed across the stack and helped shape a responsible pilot ready product.
stories.result: The pilot reduced time to treatment by 35 percent, which made the work especially meaningful to me.

stories.title: Growing NSBE
stories.situation: I am also proud of the impact of my work in NSBE at Grambling.
stories.task: My goal was to help students feel that engineering opportunities were real and accessible.
stories.action: Through steady organizing and outreach, I helped support a stronger community and meaningful partnerships.
stories.result: Seeing the chapter grow past 300 members felt tangible and lasting.

stories.title: Useful Tools
stories.situation: I am proud of projects like VisualizeMyAlgorithm and my mini compiler because they helped real users.
stories.task: I wanted them to be more than portfolio pieces.
stories.action: I focused on clarity, usability, and technical depth so the tools would genuinely help people learn or build.
stories.result: Reaching more than 1000 learners on one project and more than 50 developers on another showed that the work was useful.

question: What is your biggest strength and area of growth?

stories.title: Learning gRPC Fast
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, I had to contribute in Java, gRPC, and Protocol Buffers in a production setting.
stories.task: I needed to ramp quickly enough that my learning curve did not slow the team.
stories.action: I studied existing patterns, asked focused questions, and reinforced my understanding through end to end testing.
stories.result: I became productive quickly and shipped meaningful backend work.

stories.title: Adapting To Event Systems
stories.situation: At Hubtel, I was working with Kafka Connect, MQTT, and PostgreSQL in a real time pipeline.
stories.task: That required a stronger systems mindset than typical classroom work.
stories.action: I learned by tracing the full event flow, validating assumptions, and treating debugging as part of the learning process.
stories.result: That helped me deliver the pipeline and made me much more comfortable with event driven systems.

stories.title: Learning Through Building
stories.situation: I often learn best by building projects that force me to understand a concept end to end.
stories.task: I wanted to turn that habit into a real advantage.
stories.action: That is why I built projects in machine learning, visualization, and compiler design instead of staying with only familiar tools.
stories.result: Those experiences made me much more adaptable when new technologies appear.

question: Tell me about a time you needed information from someone who wasn't responsive.

stories.title: Content And Engineering Balance
stories.situation: At Geaux Network, the Change History feature sat between backend concerns and the practical needs of content users.
stories.task: I had to gather what each group needed without letting the feature grow out of control.
stories.action: I listened for the workflows each team cared about most and kept tradeoffs visible.
stories.result: That made the final feature more credible because it solved a real operational problem.

stories.title: Hospital Pilot Trust
stories.situation: MedDiagnose involved stakeholders with very different priorities, especially clinicians.
stories.task: I needed to communicate the system honestly so expectations stayed realistic.
stories.action: I focused on what the product could already do well, acknowledged limitations, and framed it as support for faster decisions.
stories.result: That built trust and helped us complete a valuable pilot.

stories.title: Sponsor Outreach
stories.situation: In NSBE, external sponsors cared about different outcomes than student members did.
stories.task: I had to represent student needs while speaking in a way companies would respond to.
stories.action: I tied our outreach to both community impact and talent development.
stories.result: That helped us secure partnerships while keeping the mission centered on students.
